I could not pull an official PortlandMaps parcel page for the exact 4521 address in the search results, so treat the zoning conclusion as **preliminary** until you confirm the exact tax lot on PortlandMaps or with Portland Permitting & Development. Nearby Belmont/45th parcels are listed as **CM2**, and Portland’s own zoning page says the official map must be checked for base zone, overlays, plan districts, and historic status. ([Redfin][1])

### What you can build

A **tiny house on a permanent foundation** could potentially be permitted as a small residential dwelling because **Household Living is allowed in CM2**, and CM2 allows a wide mix of residential and commercial uses. CM2 is a medium-scale corridor/center zone, generally allowing buildings up to four stories unless other limits or bonuses apply. ([Portland.gov][2])

A **tiny house on wheels** is a different category. Portland treats occupied tiny houses on wheels as **occupied recreational vehicles**. Those are generally allowed only as **one occupied RV/tiny house on wheels on a developed residential lot with a house, attached house, or manufactured home**; they are not allowed on undeveloped lots and must keep their wheels. Because Belmont/45th appears to be CM2 commercial/mixed-use, and because 4521 may be part of a commercial or multifamily building rather than a detached house lot, the tiny-house-on-wheels path is **probably not the right fit** unless the site qualifies as a developed residential lot with the required primary dwelling. ([Portland.gov][3])

### Minimum size

Portland zoning does **not** appear to set a minimum floor area for a dwelling in CM2; the CM2 chapter says there is **no required minimum lot size** for development in commercial/mixed-use zones. Oregon law defines a “small home” as a dwelling of **not more than 400 square feet**, and requires state construction standards for small homes, including sleeping loft and ladder/alternate-tread provisions. ([Portland.gov][2])

For an **ADU**, Portland sets a **maximum**, not a minimum: the ADU may be up to **75% of the living area of the main structure or 800 sq. ft., whichever is smaller**. But ADU status only works when the site has a qualifying primary house, attached house, manufactured home, or in some cases duplex; ADUs cannot be built with certain other development types including multi-dwelling structures. ([Portland.gov][4])

### Setbacks and size controls in CM2

For CM2, the core standards are roughly:

| Item                                 |                                                               CM2 standard |
| ------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------: |
| Minimum lot size                     |                                                                       None |
| Household living                     |                                                                    Allowed |
| Maximum FAR                          |                              2.5:1 base; up to 4:1 with qualifying bonuses |
| Base height                          |                                                                      45 ft |
| Street lot-line minimum setback      |                                                 None, except special cases |
| Street lot-line maximum setback      |                                                           10 ft, generally |
| Setback from residentially zoned lot |                        10 ft when abutting RF–RM4/RMP/IR residential zones |
| Building coverage                    | 100% in the Inner Pattern Area; 85% in Eastern/Western/River Pattern Areas |
| Minimum landscaped area              |                                                                        15% |
| Residential outdoor area             |                                                                   Required |

These are from the CM2 row in Portland Zoning Code Chapter 33.130. Some street/civic-corridor or adjacent-residential rules can change the setback, and overlays may add design or historic review. ([Portland.gov][2])

For a **detached ADU**, Portland’s ADU-specific rules are different: detached ADUs must be **40 ft from the front lot line or behind the rear wall of the main structure**, max **20 ft high outside required setbacks** and **15 ft within required setbacks**, no extra parking is required, and the detached ADU’s building coverage cannot exceed the primary structure’s coverage. ([Portland.gov][4])

### Utility connections

For a permitted dwelling/ADU on foundation, expect normal residential utility review:

Water can be served by a **shared meter** with the main structure or by a **separate meter**. A shared meter requires sizing based on the combined fixtures of both units; a separate meter serves only the ADU and has separate Water Bureau installation fees. The private connection from the back side of the meter may require a plumbing permit. ([Portland.gov][5])

Sanitary sewer may be shared if the existing sewer is conforming and sized correctly, but if there are **3 or more dwelling units** on the property, Portland notes a **6-inch sewer lateral in the right-of-way** is needed. A separate sewer connection requires added permits and fees. Stormwater generally must be managed on-site, and a stormwater facility is required if the project creates **500+ sq. ft. of impervious surface**. ([Portland.gov][5])

For a **tiny house on wheels**, Portland requires electrical service through a dedicated outlet/service pedestal or the primary dwelling, minimum **20-amp GFCI-protected dedicated circuit**; a new circuit requires an electrical permit. If the unit has internal plumbing, it must connect to potable water and sanitary sewer; sewer connection through a cleanout requires a plumbing permit. ([Portland.gov][3])

### Permits you should expect

For a foundation-built tiny house or ADU, you would normally need a **building permit**, site plan, architectural plans, structural plans as applicable, and trade permits for plumbing, electrical, and mechanical work. Portland says all ADU construction projects need a site plan, and new detached ADUs follow the New Single Family Residence guidelines. Applications are submitted through **Development Hub PDX**. ([Portland.gov][6])

Also check for **tree protection/removal permits**, erosion control, street-use permits if construction affects the sidewalk or street, address assignment, and possible design/historic/overlay review. Portland notes trees over 12 inches may require protective measures, all permits require final inspection approval, and ADU addresses are assigned with unit letters. ([Portland.gov][6])

### Bottom line for 4521 SE Belmont

A **permanent, code-compliant small dwelling** may be possible in CM2, but whether it can be just one tiny house depends on the exact tax lot, existing building/use, minimum-density rules, available buildable area, utilities, and overlays. A **tiny house on wheels** is likely harder on this Belmont property unless it qualifies under Portland’s occupied-RV rules for a developed residential lot with a primary house.

Before spending on design, confirm these four items with PortlandMaps/PPD: exact tax lot for 4521, whether it is indeed **CM2**, whether any **design/historic/plan district overlays** apply, and whether the existing use is a house/duplex/multifamily/commercial building.

[4]: https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-code-overview/adu-zoning "Accessory dwelling unit (ADU) zoning requirements | Portland.gov"
[5]: https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/adu-water-and-sewer-connections "Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) Connections: Water, Sanitary, and Storm Sewer | Portland.gov"

Based on the surrounding block (the 4400-4500 stretch of Belmont includes the new 65-unit "Sunnyside Belmont" apartment complex at 4405 and similar density), this stretch of Belmont was rezoned **Commercial Mixed Use (CM2)** under Portland's "Centers and Corridors" strategy. **This distinction matters a lot:**
- If your lot fronts Belmont and is **CM2**: you can build small multi-unit residential by right, with smaller setbacks, no minimum lot size for residential, heights up to ~45 ft. The "ADU" framework isn't even the most efficient path; you could just build a small primary dwelling.

I'll answer assuming R2.5 since that's the more constrained case (and most "tiny house on a residential lot" questions); the CM2 case is more permissive everywhere it differs.

## What "tiny house" actually means here

There are three legal pathways, and Portland treats them very differently:

**1. Tiny house on wheels (THOW).** Portland classifies these as **RVs**, not buildings. You're allowed **one occupied RV/THOW per residential lot that has a primary dwelling**. It must remain mobile, sit on a paved surface, be at least 5 ft from the main house, and connect to utilities properly. It cannot legally be your primary residence and cannot be an ADU.

**2. Foundation-built tiny house under 400 sq ft.** Falls under Oregon's **Small Home Specialty Code** (SHSC), which is more flexible than the standard residential code. Built on a foundation, permitted as a regular dwelling.

**3. Foundation-built ADU (any size up to the cap).** This is the most common "tiny house" path. Built on a permanent foundation under the Oregon Residential Specialty Code (ORSC), often with Appendix Q for small-house allowances (smaller stairs, lofts, etc.).

Most people who say "build a tiny house" mean #2 or #3. The rest of this answer assumes that.

## ADU rules (R2.5, 2025/2026)

**Maximum size:** 75% of the main dwelling's living area, OR 800 sq ft (for lots ≤5,000 sq ft), OR 1,000 sq ft (for lots >5,000 sq ft); whichever is less. Basement ADUs in primary structures over 5 years old can be up to 1,000 sq ft regardless. Walls thicker than standard, garage areas, low-ceiling areas (under 6'8") and inaccessible spaces don't count toward the cap.

**Number allowed:** Up to **two ADUs per lot** (typically one attached/internal + one detached) on lots that meet minimum size and front a city-maintained street. Recent 2025 code consolidation made this clearer in Chapter 33.205.

**Height:** 20 ft outside required setbacks; 15 ft inside required setbacks; exterior wall height capped at 10 ft within the setback area.

**Setbacks:**
- Detached ADU front: **40 ft from front lot line** OR behind the rear wall of the main house
- Side/rear: **5 ft minimum** (may be larger depending on zone)

**Coverage limits:** Detached ADU building coverage cannot exceed the primary structure's coverage. All accessory structures combined cannot exceed **20% of the site**.

**Design standards** kick in only if the ADU is taller than 15 ft (siding patterns, roof pitch matching primary house, etc.). A 1-story ADU under 15 ft skips this.

## Utility connections

You can either share existing water/sewer with the main house or run separate connections. Separate is usually preferable if you'll rent the unit (cleaner accounting, easier to install sub-meters, simpler if you sell).

**Specific things to budget for at this address:**
- **Sewer connection or upgrade.** Older Belmont/Sunnyside houses (many are 1900-1920 vintage) sometimes still have decommissioned cesspools or older sewer laterals; BES (Bureau of Environmental Services) may require lateral inspection or replacement.
- **Stormwater management.** New impervious surface usually triggers on-site stormwater requirements (drywell, swale, or similar).
- **Water meter.** May need an upsize if you're adding fixtures.
- **Electrical service.** Older homes often have 100A service; an ADU often pushes you to a 200A panel upgrade.
- **Hazardous-site review.** Required for flood, steep slope, or landslide. Inner Sunnyside is mostly flat and not flood-prone, but check anyway.

## Permits required

- **Building permit** (mandatory for all ADUs and primary dwellings)
- **Electrical permit** (must be pulled by a licensed electrical contractor; you cannot DIY this even as the homeowner)
- **Plumbing permit**
- **Mechanical permit** (if HVAC is involved)

You can submit all of these together for a single permit number, or stagger them. Plans go through zoning review and structural review, then you schedule inspections during build.

**Free pre-approved ADU plans:** The city offers four pre-approved detached ADU designs (gable/shed roof × slab/crawlspace) at no cost. Using them speeds permitting significantly because the structural review is already done. They won't fit every lot but worth checking first.

## Costs and incentives that might surprise you

- **System Development Charges (SDCs)** can run $10,000-$20,000+ for a new ADU. Portland offers a **full SDC waiver** if you record a covenant agreeing to **no short-term rentals (Airbnb/VRBO) for 10 years**. This is a huge financial lever most people miss.
- **There's a $15,000 incentive program** for ADU owners who commit to long-term rental and no STR. Worth checking eligibility.
- **Property taxes will go up** (Multnomah County reassesses based on the new value); rough rule of thumb is 1.0-1.3% of added improvement value annually.
- **Permit/design/build cost** for a typical 600-800 sq ft detached ADU in Portland currently runs $200,000-$350,000 turnkey; tiny-house-style under 400 sq ft can come in lower but the permit/utility/site-prep costs are roughly the same regardless of size, so cost-per-square-foot gets worse the smaller you go.

## How to verify the zoning yourself in under a minute

1. Go to **portlandmaps.com**
2. Type "4521 SE Belmont" in the search bar (top right)
3. Click your property on the map
4. Scroll to the "Zoning" section. You'll see Base Zone (CM2, R2.5, R5, etc.) and any Overlays (lowercase letters like d, h, a)

The overlay codes to watch for:
- **d** (design overlay): adds design review for new construction; Belmont has been getting these added
- **a** (alternative design density): tweaks density rules
- **h** (historic): triggers historic resource review
- An overlay map showing this stretch likely got a "d" overlay added recently

While you're there, check **Permits** (cesspool/oil tank history) and **Utilities** (sewer line type, age). The "Environmental" section flags any hazard areas.

## My recommendation for next steps

1. Verify zoning on portlandmaps.com (1 minute).
2. If R2.5 or R5: book a **free 15-minute appointment with a city planner** at portland.gov; they'll review your specific lot and tell you exactly what's allowed. This is genuinely free and they're helpful.
3. If CM2: skip the ADU framework entirely; talk to a planner about small-format multi-unit residential, which gives you more flexibility.
4. Pull a soil/cesspool/tank report from DEQ's LUST database before designing anything (older Sunnyside lots commonly have decommissioned oil tanks; remediation can run $5K-$30K and surprises mid-project are expensive).
5. Look at the city's pre-approved ADU plans before paying for custom design.

One last thing: I noticed you said "my lot," but I don't have any context confirming you own this property. If you're evaluating it pre-purchase rather than for an existing parcel you own, the same answer applies, but you'll also want title work, a survey, and an early-assistance meeting before close.

Yes, you can likely build or place a tiny house on your lot at **4521 SE Belmont St** in Portland, but it depends on the details of your project (permanent foundation vs. on wheels), the property's specific zoning, lot characteristics, and an existing primary dwelling. Portland is relatively permissive for small secondary housing options like **Accessory Dwelling Units (ADUs)** or **tiny houses on wheels (THOWs)** treated as occupied recreational vehicles.

**Important first step**: Determine the exact base zoning, lot size, existing structures, overlays (e.g., historic, environmental), and any constraints using official tools. The best resources are:

- **PortlandMaps.com** (search by address for zoning, aerial view, permits history, and property details).
- Multnomah County property search (for ownership, lot size, and tax info).
- Contact Portland Permitting & Development (formerly BDS) at 503-823-4000 or via their Development Hub for site-specific review.

SE Belmont Street runs through neighborhoods like Sunnyside (inner SE Portland), which are typically single-dwelling or multi-dwelling residential zones (e.g., R5, R7, or similar). These generally allow ADUs.

### Zoning and General Requirements
Portland's Zoning Code (Title 33) governs this. Most residential zones (single-dwelling like R2.5–R20 or multi-dwelling) allow **one ADU** accessory to an existing house. Some zones or lot conditions may allow two ADUs. ADUs are not allowed on lots with triplexes/fourplexes or certain other multi-unit setups without meeting extra rules.

- **Detached ADUs** (a common path for a "tiny house" on foundation) are allowed in single-dwelling zones (RF, R20, R10, R7, R5, R2.5) and others.
- Permanent tiny homes on a foundation are typically treated as ADUs and must comply with residential building code (Oregon Residential Specialty Code).
- Tiny houses on wheels (THOWs) are regulated separately as "occupied recreational vehicles" and can be placed on a lot *with* an existing primary house (not on vacant lots).

**Minimum lot size** varies by zone and housing type (e.g., often 2,500–5,000+ sq ft for ADUs in common R zones; check your property). There is **no strict minimum size** for the ADU/tiny house itself—tiny homes are feasible as long as other standards are met.

### Size Limits (for ADUs)
- Maximum: 75% of the primary dwelling's living area **or 800 sq ft**, whichever is smaller. On larger lots (>5,000 sq ft in some cases), it may go up to 1,000 sq ft.
- Basements or internal conversions may have different allowances.
- No minimum floor area is specified in zoning (though building code requires habitable space standards like ceiling heights, egress, etc.).

Detached accessory structures (including ADUs) have combined coverage limits (e.g., often ≤20% of the lot or similar, depending on zone; cannot exceed primary structure coverage in some cases).

### Setbacks and Placement
**For detached ADUs**:
- Must be at least 40 feet from the front lot line **or** located behind the rear wall of the primary house.
- Typically match the primary structure's side and rear setbacks (often 5 ft side/rear in many residential zones, but confirm for your property).
- Minimum 6 ft between primary house and ADU in some cases.
- Height: 20 ft max outside setbacks; 15 ft max within setbacks. Taller ones (>15 ft) have design matching requirements (roof pitch, materials, etc.).

**For THOWs/occupied RVs**:
- Must be on a developed lot *with* a primary house.
- Parked behind the front facade of the primary house, on a compliant surface (paved if motorized).
- At least 5 ft from the primary dwelling.
- Wheels must stay on (remains mobile; not a permanent structure).

Front-yard placement is generally restricted.

### Utility Connections
- **ADUs (permanent)**: Must connect to city water, sewer, and electrical services. Can often share the primary house's connections (with proper sizing/capacity checks and permits). Separate meters or upgrades may be needed. Plumbing/electrical/mechanical permits are required. System Development Charges (SDCs) may apply but are sometimes waived or reduced for ADUs.
- **THOWs**: Electrical via a dedicated 20-amp GFCI outlet (permit if new circuit). Sewer via cleanout (plumbing permit) if it has internal plumbing. Water can use a hose bibb or similar. If no internal plumbing, occupants must access bathroom/shower in the primary house. No full building permit for the unit itself, but utility work requires permits.

Off-grid setups are generally not allowed due to city utility and code rules.

### Permit Requirements
- **Permanent tiny house/ADU**: Requires a **building permit** + trade permits (electrical, plumbing, mechanical). Submit via Portland's Development Hub (online). Includes plan review, inspections, and compliance with energy code, egress, insulation, etc. Process can take weeks to months. Fees vary (thousands of dollars typical). Pre-approved ADU plans may speed things up.
- **THOW**: No building permit for the structure itself (if it meets RV/tiny home mobility rules). Only electrical/plumbing permits for connections if needed.
- Other: Check for tree protection, stormwater, or historic overlay rules. Enforcement is often complaint-driven, but compliance is strongly recommended to avoid issues.

**Design standards** for detached ADUs >15 ft tall include matching the primary home's style (materials, roof, etc.), with more flexibility for smaller/shorter units (<500 sq ft or <15 ft in recent updates). No extra parking required for the ADU.

### Recommendations
1. **Look up your property** immediately on PortlandMaps.com and Multnomah County property search for zoning confirmation, lot dimensions, existing building footprint, and prior permits.
2. Review full details in Portland Zoning Code Chapter 33.205 (ADUs) and related sections.
3. Consult a professional (architect, designer experienced in Portland ADUs, or the city's Permitting & Development staff) for a pre-application review. Tiny home builders familiar with Portland rules can also help.
4. Consider whether a foundation-based ADU or mobile THOW better fits your goals—ADUs offer more permanence but more regulation; THOWs are simpler but restricted in use/placement.

Rules can have nuances based on your exact zone, lot shape, neighborhood character, or overlays (e.g., historic districts near Belmont). Always verify with official sources, as codes evolve (Portland has updated ADU rules to encourage smaller units). For the most accurate advice, reach out to the City of Portland directly with your address.

Yes, you can build a tiny house or place a Tiny House on Wheels (THOW) on your lot at **4521 SE Belmont St**. This property is located in the **Sunnyside neighborhood**, which is within the City of Portland's jurisdiction and generally zoned as **R2.5 (Residential Multi-Dwelling 1)** or similar residential density, which supports Accessory Dwelling Units (ADUs).

### 1. Zoning & Placement
In Portland, tiny houses are typically regulated in one of two ways: as a permanent **Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU)** on a foundation or as an **Occupied RV/Tiny House on Wheels**.
* **Location:** The tiny house must be located in the **side or rear yard**, behind the front wall of the primary house. It cannot be placed on a vacant lot without a primary residence.
* **Setbacks:** * **Front:** Must be at least **40 feet** from the front lot line or behind the rear wall of the main house.
    * **Side/Rear:** Generally **5 feet** from property lines. If the unit is within the required setback, the maximum height is limited to **15 feet**.
    * **Between Buildings:** There must be a minimum **5-foot separation** from the main house and any other structures (garages/sheds).

### 2. Size & Height Requirements
* **Minimum Size:** For a permanent ADU, the minimum size is **200 square feet**.
* **Maximum Size:** It cannot exceed **75% of the primary house's living area** or **800 square feet**, whichever is smaller.
* **Building Coverage:** The tiny house cannot exceed the footprint of the primary house, and all accessory structures combined cannot cover more than **20% of the total lot area**.
* **Height:** Max **20 feet** if outside setbacks; max **15 feet** if within setbacks.

### 3. Utility Connections
* **Sanitation:** Must be connected to the City’s sewer system or an approved septic system. Use of "blackwater" holding tanks is not permitted for permanent occupancy.
* **Electricity:** Must have a permanent legal connection. **Generators are strictly prohibited** for powering a tiny house in a residential zone.
* **Water:** Must be connected to a legal water source (typically shared with the primary residence).

### 4. Permits & Design
* **Permit Types:** You will need a **Building Permit** and relevant trade permits (Electrical, Plumbing, Mechanical). 
* **Permanent vs. Wheels:** * **On Foundation:** Must follow the **Oregon Residential Specialty Code** (specifically Appendix Q for tiny houses).
    * **On Wheels:** Treated as an "Occupied RV." It must be registered with the DMV and have a manufacturing certification (like RVIA).
* **Design Standards:** If the tiny house is taller than 15 feet, it must "visually match" the primary residence in terms of roof pitch, siding materials, and trim.
* **SDC Waivers:** Portland offers a **System Development Charge (SDC) waiver** that can save you several thousand dollars if you agree not to use the unit as a short-term rental (Airbnb) for 10 years.

### Recommended Next Steps
Because regulations can change based on specific lot overlays (such as historic or environmental zones), you should verify your exact site constraints on **PortlandMaps**. You can also schedule a free **15-minute appointment** with a Portland City Planner to review your specific site plan for the Belmont St lot.

All four tools hit the address-specific cap (usefulness ceiling of 2) because none successfully retrieved the actual PortlandMaps parcel record for 4521 SE Belmont St.

---

## Standout Responses

**ChatGPT** and **Grok** tie for best citation quality. ChatGPT's distinguishing strength is that it retrieved and linked actual Portland.gov PDF documents (the Chapter 33.130 commercial zones PDF, the ADU zoning page, the occupied RV page), which means its citations are anchored to source materials a homeowner could open and verify. It is also the only tool that correctly flagged the commercial zoning question — 4521 SE Belmont sits on a corridor where CM2 is plausible, and getting the base zone wrong changes every downstream answer. ChatGPT identified this uncertainty honestly rather than defaulting to a residential zone assumption.

Grok earns its 3/3 citation score with the lowest accuracy flag count (12) among the section-citing tools, suggesting its specific claims are slightly more defensible than ChatGPT's 14-flag list. Its response is cleanly structured and consistently directed users to PortlandMaps for ground truth rather than projecting false confidence.

**Claude** falls to a 2/3 citation score despite naming Chapter 33.205 because the majority of its numerical claims (setbacks, heights, SDC waiver terms) were asserted without section references. It also contains a disqualifying data integrity problem: the opening paragraphs reference stored context about a different user ("C", "Yex Labs LLC") — a cross-session hallucination or prompt injection artifact that would be alarming in a public-facing city service context.

**Gemini** is the weakest on citations. It names only Oregon Residential Specialty Code Appendix Q (a state-level document, not Portland city code) and provides no Title 33 section numbers at all. Several of its specific figures appear drawn from pre-2021 Portland ADU rules that were substantially reformed under the Residential Infill Project, making it the most likely to mislead a homeowner acting on outdated information.

---

## Unique Findings

**ChatGPT only — CM2 commercial zoning identification:**
ChatGPT is the only tool that raised the possibility that 4521 SE Belmont may be in a commercial/mixed-use (CM2) zone rather than a single-dwelling residential zone. This matters enormously: if the parcel is CM2, the ADU rules, setback tables, coverage limits, and FAR calculations differ entirely from the residential code all other tools cited. A homeowner who proceeds on an R5/R7 assumption and discovers a CM2 designation at permit intake would need to restart their planning process. ChatGPT flagged the source of its inference (a Redfin listing — non-authoritative) and told the user to verify, which is the correct response to this uncertainty.

**ChatGPT only — 6-inch sewer lateral threshold for 3+ units:**
ChatGPT noted that a 6-inch sewer lateral is required when there are three or more dwelling units on a property. This is relevant if the parcel already has an ADU or if the owner is considering a second ADU, and it is a utility cost that can surprise applicants who assume a standard connection suffices.

**ChatGPT only — Pattern area classification within CM2:**
ChatGPT noted that CM2 building coverage varies by pattern area (100% in Inner Pattern Area vs. 85% in Eastern/Western/River areas), and that the pattern area for this specific address was not confirmed. No other tool raised pattern area classification as a variable.

**Claude only — $15,000 long-term rental incentive program:**
Claude mentioned a city incentive program offering $15,000 for ADUs committed to long-term rental. No other tool raised this. If current and accurate, it is directly relevant to a homeowner's financial planning. Its accuracy should be verified — the flag count includes a note that the dollar amount and program availability are unverified.

**Claude only — Pre-approved ADU design plans:**
Claude noted that the city offers pre-approved detached ADU designs, which can reduce permitting time and design cost. This is a practical accelerator for homeowners that no other tool mentioned.

**Grok only — 6-foot minimum separation between primary house and ADU:**
Grok specified a 6-foot minimum separation between the primary structure and the ADU, distinct from side/rear setbacks. ChatGPT and Claude cited a 5-foot side/rear setback but did not address inter-structure separation. Gemini cited 5 feet for both. The distinction matters for siting decisions on small lots.

**Gemini only — Generator prohibition:**
Gemini stated that generators are strictly prohibited for powering a tiny house in a residential zone. No other tool raised this. If accurate, it closes off a workaround some THOW owners attempt and is worth verifying against Portland City Code.

---

## Citation Quality Comparison

**ChatGPT (3/3):** The strongest citation performance. Named Chapter 33.130 (commercial zones) and Chapter 33.205 (ADUs) by number, and linked directly to Portland.gov PDFs — not just named the titles but provided clickable URLs to the authoritative source documents. This is the behavior the hackathon challenge explicitly requires ("clear attribution to official documents").

**Grok (3/3):** Named Title 33 and Chapter 33.205 by number. Did not link to source documents but cited at section-level granularity. The 33.205 citation is consistent with Portland's known ADU code structure. A homeowner can use this to navigate the Portland Code Finder directly.

**Claude (2/3):** Named Chapter 33.205 and Oregon Residential Specialty Code with Appendix Q, which is a legitimate state-level citation for tiny home construction standards. However, the bulk of its numerical specifics (setbacks, height limits, SDC waiver duration) were stated without corresponding code sections, leaving the homeowner unable to verify the claims without independent research.

**Gemini (1/3):** The only document named is Oregon Residential Specialty Code Appendix Q. No Portland Title 33 section numbers appear anywhere in the response. A homeowner following Gemini's guidance would have no path to verify any of the Portland-specific claims against actual city code. This directly fails the hackathon's core requirement.

No tool cited Title 24 (Building Regulations), Title 25 (Plumbing), Title 26 (Electrical), or Title 27 (Heating and Ventilating) — all of which are triggered by an ADU construction permit.

---

## Qualitative Differences

**Depth and specificity:** ChatGPT produced the most numerically specific response, covering FAR, height limits, coverage ratios, sewer lateral sizing, stormwater impervious surface thresholds, and THOW electrical specifications. The tradeoff is the highest accuracy flag count (14): more claims means more claims that require verification. Grok achieved comparable specificity with fewer flags, suggesting more conservative claim-making. Claude matched depth on program details (incentives, pre-approved plans) but fell short on code section attribution. Gemini offered the shallowest coverage — its flag count is lower only because it made fewer claims.

**Confidence calibration:** All four tools appropriately hedged the zoning classification, which is the correct behavior given the PortlandMaps retrieval failure. ChatGPT was most transparent about the source of its zoning inference (a Redfin listing), which is important for a city service context where users should understand the basis for any claim. Gemini stated the zone as "R2.5 (Residential Multi-Dwelling 1)" with the lightest hedging language — this is both the least accurate zone label (R2.5 refers to minimum lot size, not a multi-dwelling classification in Portland's schema) and the most overconfident presentation.

**Approach to the THOW vs. ADU distinction:** All four tools correctly identified that "tiny house" means different things under Portland code. ChatGPT was clearest that the THOW path is probably not appropriate for a likely CM2 commercial parcel. Grok and Claude treated the distinction as parallel tracks. Gemini covered both but used the outdated 75% proportionality size cap that Portland eliminated.

**Data integrity:** Claude's cross-session hallucination about "user C at Yex Labs LLC" is a disqualifying reliability signal for a public-facing city service. A resident accessing a Portland city tool and seeing references to a different user's stored context would lose trust in the entire response.

**Recency of underlying training data:** Gemini shows the most evidence of relying on pre-2021 ADU rules (75% proportionality cap, 200 sq ft minimum, 40-foot front setback phrasing). Portland's Residential Infill Project substantially revised ADU regulations in 2020–2021. Tools whose training data reflects pre-reform summaries will consistently mislead homeowners on size and setback questions.

---

## Gaps

The following code requirements and citations are relevant to this question but were not adequately addressed by any tool:

**Historic resource and design overlay checks:** Southeast Portland contains Conservation Districts and Historic Resource Overlay Zones that impose additional design review requirements on ADUs. None of the four tools confirmed whether 4521 SE Belmont is subject to these overlays, and none cited the relevant overlay sections of Title 33.

**Title 24, 25, 26, 27 permit requirements:** Every tool discussed the zoning permit path but none cited the building code titles that actually govern ADU construction: Title 24 (Building Regulations), Title 25 (Plumbing), Title 26 (Electrical), and Title 27 (Heating and Ventilating). A homeowner permitting an ADU will interact with all of these, and a code guidance tool should name them.

**System Development Charges (SDCs) quantification:** All tools acknowledged SDCs exist and can be waived, but none provided even an order-of-magnitude cost estimate or named the applicable utilities (BES, PWB, Parks, Transportation). For a homeowner doing financial planning, this is a significant gap — SDCs frequently exceed $10,000 for a new dwelling unit.

**Pre-application conference requirement:** Portland Permitting & Development offers (and in some cases requires) pre-application conferences for ADU projects. No tool mentioned this as a first step, which means homeowners may proceed to formal application without an early review that could catch site-specific issues.

**Residential Infill Project (RIP) expanded ADU allowances:** Portland's 2021 RIP amendments allow up to two ADUs on most residential lots and in some cases allow ADUs on lots without a primary dwelling. Claude mentioned RIP by name but did not explain its specific expansions. No other tool named it. This is among the most significant recent changes to Portland ADU law and its omission leaves homeowners with an incomplete picture.

**Flood zone and environmental overlay verification:** PortlandMaps would reveal whether this parcel has a Flood Hazard Area or Environmental/Greenway overlay. No tool mentioned these as variables, despite their being standard due diligence items in a parcel-level code query.

claude.response:
Let me verify the parcel geometry and pull current Portland zoning rules to give you accurate setbacks and ADU rules.I have enough property data and current Portland code to give you a real answer. Let me put together a parcel diagram showing what fits.Short answer: yes, this lot is well-suited to a tiny house. R7 zone, 8,276 sq ft (above the 7,000 sq ft minimum), no flood zone, no landslide hazard, no environmental overlays, no historic designation. You have two genuinely different paths, and which one you pick changes the cost, timeline, and effort by an order of magnitude.

## The two paths

**Path A: Detached ADU on a permanent foundation.** This is what most people mean by "tiny house" when they want a rental or guest unit that adds appraisal value. It is a real building, permitted under Zoning Code Chapter 33.205, with a foundation, full code review, and System Development Charges. Higher cost, more permitting, but it adds real estate value and can be financed/insured like a home.

**Path B: Park Model RV / Tiny House on Wheels (THOW).** Portland is genuinely one of the easiest cities in the US for this; under the 2021 Shelter to Housing Continuum (S2HC) rules, one occupied recreational vehicle, including a tiny house on wheels, is allowed on a residential lot with a house. No ADU permit, no SDCs, but you cannot legally rent it short-term, it doesn't add appraised value the way an ADU does, and it has to stay technically classified as an RV (wheels stay on, sits on piers, ANSI A119.5 build standard).

## What the lot allows for an ADU (Path A)

The lot is ~75 ft on 174th by ~108 ft deep. Setbacks come from the R7 base zone and the ADU chapter combined:

- **Front (east, on 174th):** 10 ft base zone setback for any building. For a *detached* ADU specifically, detached ADUs must be set back 40 feet from the front lot line, or located behind the rear wall of the main structure. That second clause is the practical one; if your main house's rear wall is 35 ft back, the ADU just has to start behind that line.
- **Sides (north and south):** 5 ft.
- **Rear (west):** 5 ft.
- **Maximum ADU height:** 20 feet outside the required setbacks; 15 feet inside required setbacks.
- **Maximum size:** 75% of the living area of the main house, or 800 square feet, whichever is smaller. With your main house at 960 sq ft, the cap is **720 sq ft**. (Basement ADUs in a primary structure 5+ years old are exempt from this cap, but that's not relevant for a detached build.)
- **Building coverage:** all detached accessory structures combined must not cover more than 20% of the site; here that's 1,655 sq ft maximum across all sheds, garages, and the ADU footprint together.
- **Design standards:** for detached ADUs taller than 15 feet, exterior compatibility standards apply (siding pattern, roof, etc.). Stay at or under 15 ft and you skip those entirely, which is one of the underrated reasons single-story ADUs are popular here.
- **Parking:** none required for the ADU itself, though existing required parking for the main house must be maintained or replaced.

A nice quirk of the post-RIP code: in the R7, R5, and R2.5 zones, two ADUs are typically allowed on a site with a house if the lot meets a minimum lot area of 5,000 sq ft for R7. Your lot at 8,276 sq ft qualifies. So you could plan one detached ADU now and a second (basement conversion, or attached) later. If you build two ADUs, one has to meet visitable-unit standards (no-step entrance, accessible bath, 200+ sq ft accessible living area).

## Utility gotchas worth knowing before you call a contractor

This is where Centennial trips people up:

- **Water is Rockwood Water PUD, not Portland Water Bureau.** Phone (503) 665-4179. Connection fees, meter sizing, and timelines are theirs, not the City's. Confirm a new meter or upsized service is even available before designing.
- **Sewer is Portland BES.** New ADUs typically tap the existing house lateral but BES will want a sewer permit and may require a video inspection of your lateral if it's old (1947 build implies the line may be original clay).
- **Power:** PGE is the utility; a 200A panel upgrade on the main house is often cheaper than a separate ADU service.
- **Gas:** NW Natural; whether you run gas to the ADU is a design choice that affects insulation and ventilation requirements.

Centennial is inside the Portland city limits but in the **Reynolds School District**, **Portland Police East Precinct**, **Portland Fire (shared with Gresham, Station 31, ~1,200 ft north)**. The seismic hazard summary for the parcel: Cascadia subduction zone shows "Very Strong" perceived shaking at moderate damage potential, Portland Hills Fault similar; no liquefaction zone, no steep slope, no special flood hazard. So the foundation is conventional but you'll want a competent structural engineer; nothing exotic.

## Fees and permits to plan for (Path A)

- **Building permit + plan review:** several thousand, scaled to project valuation.
- **System Development Charges (SDCs):** these are the painful ones, covering water, sewer, transportation, parks, schools. For a new detached ADU in Portland, expect roughly **$15,000–$30,000** in combined SDCs depending on size and current schedules. The City has used ADU SDC waivers in the past; that program has expired and rebooted multiple times, so verify current status before pricing the project.
- **Electrical permit:** you must hire a licensed electrical contractor to do the electrical work on an ADU; you cannot do it yourself even as the owner.
- **Pre-approved plans:** the City offers pre-approved building plans for detached ADUs; if you use them, you submit those with your permit application. Reviewers move faster, and you save on architect fees. Three options are published (gable-roof crawlspace, shed-roof slab-on-grade, shed-roof crawlspace). Worth looking at before commissioning custom design.

## What the THOW path actually looks like (Path B)

If you'd rather skip the SDCs and full permit cycle, the THOW rules are tight but workable:

- One occupied RV/THOW maximum on the lot, alongside the existing house.
- Must be parked behind the front area of the primary house, wheels stay on, set on cement blocks or metal piers and leveled.
- Permitted electrical hookup (RV-style outdoor connection), a potable water hookup, and an RV-style sewer tie-in to the main house lateral installed by a licensed plumber.
- **Long-term occupancy is allowed; short-term rental (Airbnb-style) is not** under the THOW framework.
- The unit needs to be built to ANSI A119.5 (Park Model RV) or be a registered RV/travel trailer; a custom-built tiny house that doesn't meet those standards is in a much grayer zone.

Total cost typically lands in the $50–110k range for a quality Park Model plus pad, hookups, and delivery, vs. $200–400k+ for a built-on-site ADU in current Portland numbers.

## The one thing I'd do first

Before anything else, book a **free 15-minute planner appointment** with Portland Permitting & Development. It's not advertised well; people pay $3k+ in design fees and only then discover an easement or utility issue that nukes the project. The planner will pull your specific parcel up on their internal system, confirm there are no issues my data didn't catch (private easements aren't always in the public layers), and tell you which of the two paths actually pencils for your existing house position. Phone the BDS planning line at 503-823-7300 weekday mornings and ask for either a 15-minute appointment or an Early Assistance meeting.

A second small thing: your house at 960 sq ft is the constraint that's pinning the ADU at 720 sq ft max. If you ever expand the main house (even modestly), the ADU cap rises with it; a 1,067 sq ft main house unlocks the full 800 sq ft cap. Worth knowing if you have any addition plans for the main house, since combining permits often saves on review fees.

One disclaimer worth flagging: the assessor record I pulled lists owners other than what your "my lot" phrasing suggests; that's often just a recording lag after a sale, but if this is a property you're researching for purchase rather than already own, the answer above is still accurate, and the two paths apply regardless. None of the analysis depends on you being the owner of record.

R5 matters because its required setbacks are typically **10 ft front / 5 ft side / 5 ft rear**. ([Portland.gov][1])

### Fence height limits

| Area                           |                   Zoning max height | Practical note                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| ------------------------------ | ----------------------------------: | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Front yard / front setback** |                          **3.5 ft** | Applies in the required front building setback, or between the front lot line and front building line, whichever is less. For R5, that usually means the first **10 ft** from the front lot line, unless the house is closer. ([Portland.gov][1]) |

For a normal side lot line, assume **8 ft zoning max**. ([Portland.gov][1])                                                                                    |
| **Back yard / rear setback**   |                            **8 ft** | Same as side: **8 ft** in required rear setbacks unless special pedestrian-connection rules apply. ([Portland.gov][1])                                                                                                                            |
| **Outside required setbacks**  | Up to the regular zone height limit | In R5, regular structure height is generally **30 ft**, but a “fence” that tall becomes a very different permitting/engineering problem. Portland says fences not in required setbacks use the regular zone height limit. ([Portland.gov][1])     |

### Permit threshold vs zoning limit

Portland’s building-permit threshold is separate from the zoning height limit. A **wood fence over 7 ft** needs a building permit; **masonry/concrete over 4 ft** needs a permit; **woven wire/chain link over 8 ft** needs a permit. Height includes lattice, posts, or other material added on top. ([Portland.gov][2])

So: **an 8 ft side/rear wood fence may be allowed by zoning, but still needs a building permit** because it is over 7 ft.

### Overlays / Irvington issue

The main overlay question is the **Irvington Historic District**. The official historic district is generally bounded by **NE Fremont, NE Broadway, NE 7th, and NE 27th**, with a small extension around part of NE 28th; the historic-district text says the boundary jogs near NE Klickitat and then runs north along/near NE 27th. That makes **2815 NE Klickitat, near NE 28th/29th, likely outside the Irvington Historic District**, even if people casually call the area Irvington/near Irvington. ([Portland.gov][3])

If the parcel is **not in a historic/design/conservation overlay**, the overlay should **not change the fence height limits**. If PortlandMaps shows it inside a historic resource/district after all, the height numbers above may still be the baseline, but **historic review/design compatibility could affect materials, placement, or visibility**, especially for front-yard work. Portland’s own fence page specifically says overlay zones can add requirements and recommends checking Planning & Zoning for design/greenway/overlay issues. ([Portland.gov][2])

**Bottom line:** likely **3.5 ft in the front setback**, **8 ft in side/rear setbacks**, with a **permit needed for wood over 7 ft**. I would verify the parcel in PortlandMaps before building, mainly to confirm whether any historic/overlay flag attaches to the exact tax lot.

[1]: https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone.pdf "Title 33, Planning and Zoning, Chapter 33.110 Single-Dwelling Zones"
[2]: https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/fence-permits "Fence Residential Building Permits | Portland.gov"

These are the standard allowances in many single-dwelling zones (e.g., R5, R7). Fences are measured from the grade immediately under the fence, including any posts, trellises, lattice, or toppings.

  - Woven wire/chain-link fences ≤8 feet high.

# AI Tool Comparison: Portland Fence Height Query (2815 NE Klickitat St)

## Score Summary

| Tool | Usefulness (0-3) | Citation Quality (0-3) | Accuracy Flags | Key Strength |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ChatGPT (5.5) | 2/3 | 3/3 | 11 | Direct URLs to Title 33.110 PDF, fence permit page, and Irvington Historic District nomination document |

| Grok | 2/3 | 2/3 | 12 | Clear permit-threshold breakdown by material type (wood/masonry/chain-link) |
| Gemini | 2/3 | 0/3 | 10 | Most decisive on the Irvington Historic District overlay implication (mandatory Historic Resource Review for any new fence) |

## Standout Responses

**ChatGPT earned the only 3/3 citation score** by linking directly to the authoritative Title 33.110 PDF, the official fence permit page, and the Irvington Historic District nomination document. This is the response that best satisfies the hackathon's core requirement of "clear references to authoritative sources." It also delivered the cleanest tabular presentation of the three height zones with the practical interpretation of each.

**Claude was strongest on substantive code knowledge** despite only 2/3 citations. It cited specific subsections by number (33.110.275 for fence standards, 33.846.060.G for Historic Resource Review, Figure 110-15 for the corner lot diagram) and uniquely surfaced edge-case rules: the pedestrian-connection ROW exception, the corner-lot flip rule, the higher-grade measurement rule for fences on retaining walls, and the bicycle-boulevard status of NE Klickitat. It was also the only tool to provide direct contact numbers for BDS planning (503-823-7300) AND PBOT ROW (503-823-7002).

**Gemini was strongest on the overlay consequence** — it was the only tool to assert clearly that within the Irvington Historic District, the standard "no permit under 7 feet" exemption does not apply and that any new fence triggers Historic Resource Review. This is the most consequential single fact for a homeowner at this address. However, its citation failure (broken googleusercontent.com placeholder links, zero Title/section numbers) makes it unreliable as standalone guidance.

## Unique Findings

- **Pedestrian-connection ROW exception** (Claude only): Side/rear fences drop from 8 ft to 3½ ft when abutting a pedestrian connection right-of-way under 30 ft wide. Matters for any lot adjacent to a public path or narrow ROW.
- **Corner lot flip rule with Figure 110-15** (Claude only): On a corner lot where the main entrance faces the side street, the homeowner can get 8 ft along the side street setback in exchange for keeping the first 10 ft from the side street lot line at 3½ ft. Directly relevant if the lot is on a corner.
- **Fence-on-retaining-wall measurement** (Claude only): Height is measured from the higher grade. A common gotcha that turns a "compliant" 6-ft fence into a code violation.
- **NE Klickitat is a designated bicycle boulevard/greenway** (Claude only): Affects PBOT ROW considerations for any fence near the front lot line.
- **One-foot-of-setback-per-extra-foot-of-height rule for fences over 8 ft** (Grok only): Captures the rule for taller fences outside required setbacks.
- **R5 regular structure height limit of 30 ft** (ChatGPT and Grok): Provides context for the zone but is tangential to fences.
- **Direct URL to Irvington Historic District nomination document** (ChatGPT only): The actual primary source defining the district boundaries, not a derivative summary.
- **Disagreement on whether the property is in the Irvington Historic District**: Claude and ChatGPT inferred the property is **outside** the district (east of NE 27th Ave); Gemini asserted it **is inside**; Grok hedged with "Irvington Conservation District (IV)" which appears to be a fabricated overlay name. This is the single highest-stakes factual disagreement in the corpus and warrants verification against PortlandMaps.

## Citation Quality Comparison

- **ChatGPT (3/3)** — Direct URLs to (a) Title 33.110 PDF, (b) the official Portland fence permit page, (c) the Irvington Historic District nomination document. Footnote-style markers in the response.
- **Claude (2/3)** — Named specific PCC sections by number (33.110.275, 33.846.060.G, Figure 110-15) but provided no URLs. Strong section-level precision, weak retrievability.
- **Grok (2/3)** — Named "Title 33" and "Chapter 33.445" (Historic District) but did not cite the actual fence-standards section (33.110.250/.275). Referenced "Brochure 3" by name without a URL. The "Irvington Conservation District (IV)" appears to be a fabricated designation — Portland uses "Historic" not "Conservation" for Irvington.
- **Gemini (0/3)** — No Title or section numbers anywhere. Embedded broken googleusercontent.com placeholder links instead of real URLs. Despite making confident, specific claims (R5, 10-ft setback, 5-ft side setback, mandatory Historic Resource Review), provided zero verifiable source for any of them.

No tool cited the wrong Title for fence standards (Title 33 is correct). Grok's "Chapter 33.445" for Irvington Historic District is plausible but not verified; the standard citation for Historic Resource Review is Chapter 33.846.

## Qualitative Differences

**Depth of code knowledge** — Claude was clearly deepest, surfacing four distinct edge cases (pedestrian ROW, corner lot flip, retaining-wall measurement, bicycle greenway) that no other tool mentioned. ChatGPT was solid but stayed close to the headline numbers. Grok and Gemini stayed at the surface.

**Confidence vs. hedging** — All four tools failed the address-specific retrieval task, but they handled the failure differently. ChatGPT and Claude were transparent: both explicitly admitted they could not pull PortlandMaps data for this parcel and were inferring zoning from neighborhood context. Grok hedged softly ("predominantly zoned R5", "nearby properties are frequently listed as R5"). Gemini asserted R5 + Irvington Historic District overlay with no hedging and no source — the most dangerous failure mode for a city-services context, because a homeowner reading Gemini's response would not know the underlying lookup was never performed.

**Approach to the overlay question** — The Irvington Historic District is the most consequential variable in this query, and the four tools split on it: Claude said "almost certainly outside" with a specific boundary rationale (east of NE 27th); ChatGPT said "likely outside" with similar reasoning; Gemini said "inside" with full confidence and described the implications; Grok hedged into a non-existent "Conservation District" label. **Presentation** — ChatGPT used a clean three-row table for the height limits, the most readable format. Claude used prose with section headers and a corner-lot diagram reference. Grok used bullets with strong permit-threshold detail. Gemini used bold callouts for the historic-overlay implications.

ChatGPT and Claude both treat citations as load-bearing (URLs and section numbers respectively); Gemini treats them as optional decoration. For a city-services tool where homeowners may act on responses without verifying, this gap is the single most important differentiator.

- **Easements on the parcel** — fences cannot be placed in utility, drainage, or pedestrian easements without consent. None of the tools mentioned this.
- **Tree protection interaction (Title 11)** — fence post installation near street trees or significant trees requires Urban Forestry review under Title 11. Relevant in Irvington's tree-canopied blocks. No tool raised it.
- **Building Code height vs. Zoning Code height** — the 7-ft wood / 4-ft masonry / 8-ft chain-link permit thresholds come from the Building Code, not Title 33. ### What that means under Title 11

If the tree is a **street tree** — in the public right-of-way / planting strip, even if it feels like your “front yard” — **a permit is always required** to remove it. Portland says all street trees require a permit before removal. ([Portland.gov][2])

| Dead/dying/dangerous, nuisance species, or within 10 ft of building |                       Permit still required if regulated size |       Usually **tree-for-tree** |

Replacement trees for private property generally must mature to at least **16 feet**, be Portland-hardy, non-invasive, and meet minimum planting size rules. ([Portland.gov][6])

### Bottom line

For a **non-Heritage front-yard private tree** at 16535 NE Everett Ct:

**You can likely remove it if either:**

1. it is **under 12" DBH**, no permit normally needed; or
2. it is **12"+ DBH**, you get a Portland Urban Forestry tree removal/replanting permit and satisfy replacement.

In Portland, the strip of grass between the sidewalk and the curb is City right-of-way, not your yard, even though you mow it. **Any tree growing in that strip, or that even straddles the property line, is legally a "Street Tree"**, not a private tree, and gets a much stricter set of rules. # What Title 11 actually requires for your property

**For a private tree (inside the property line)** at this address, under Title 11 Chapter 11.40:

- **Under 12" DBH** (diameter at 4.5 ft above ground): no permit needed, no replanting required, just remove it. You can do up to 4 healthy trees under 20" per calendar year with a permit.
- **12" or larger DBH**: a Tree Removal and Replanting Permit is required. As of July 1, 2025, the application fee is $0 (waived via PCEF funding), but the permit is still mandatory and approval is conditional.

**For a street tree (in ROW or straddling the line)**: any size requires a permit, including small ones. ### Key Rules Under Portland's Title 11 (Tree Code)
Portland regulates trees on private property primarily through **Title 11**. Here's the core framework for private trees (front yard trees on your lot count as private property trees, not street trees unless they're in the public right-of-way):

- **Permit required for removal**: A tree removal (and replanting) permit is needed for any private tree **12 inches or larger in diameter** (measured at 4.5 feet above ground, or DBH). Smaller trees generally do not require a permit for removal.
- **Heritage Trees**: These are specially designated trees of special importance due to age, size, type, historical association, or horticultural value. Any work (including pruning or removal) on a **Heritage Tree** always requires a separate **Heritage Tree Permit**, with stricter review. For private Heritage Trees, owner consent was required at designation, and it binds future owners via the property deed. Removal typically needs Urban Forestry Commission review (and sometimes City Council approval) plus a public hearing in many cases. Penalties and mitigation are higher.

- **Replacement obligations**: If removal is approved, you will generally need to replace the tree(s) on-site according to city tables (often "tree-for-tree" or "up to inch-for-inch" based on total diameter removed), plant approved species of minimum size (e.g., 1.5-inch caliper), and maintain them. Alternatively, the city may allow (or require) payment into the Tree Planting and Preservation Fund instead of on-site planting, or grant waivers/reductions in some cases (e.g., for hazardous trees). - **Limits and review**: You can typically remove up to four healthy trees per site per year under simpler Type A permits (if under 20" DBH and meeting other criteria). Removing a tree at **16535 NE Everett Ct** in the Glenfair neighborhood falls under the jurisdiction of the City of Portland’s **Title 11 (Tree Code)**. Since you are located in Portland, the rules for removal depend primarily on the tree's size and its location (whether it is on your private property or in the public right-of-way).

### 1. Heritage Tree Designations
There are **no Heritage Tree designations** for this specific property. 
* **Verification:** Heritage Trees are designated by the City Council for their age, size, or historical significance. They are marked with a small metal plaque, and owners must explicitly consent to the designation.
* **Significance:** Because no Heritage Tree exists here, you do not need to go through the rigorous "decommissioning" process required to remove one.

### 2. Permit Requirements (Title 11)
Whether you need a permit depends on two factors: the **location** of the tree and its **Diameter at Breast Height (DBH)**, which is measured $4.5$ feet above the ground.

#### **If the tree is in your Private Yard:**
* **No Permit Needed:** If the tree is **under 12 inches DBH**, you can generally remove it without a permit, provided the property is not in a specific environmental overlay zone (Glenfair residential lots typically are not).
* **Permit Required (Type A):** If the tree is **12 inches DBH or larger**, a permit is required. 
* **Large Trees (Type B):** If the tree is **20 inches DBH or larger** and healthy, the process is more involved, requiring a "Type B" permit which includes a public notice period and higher fees.

#### **If the tree is a Street Tree (Right-of-Way):**
* **Permit ALWAYS Required:** Any tree located in the public right-of-way (typically the strip between the sidewalk and the street) requires a permit for removal, **regardless of size**.
* **Standard for Approval:** For street trees, removal is usually only granted if the tree is dead, dying, dangerous, or a nuisance species.

### 3. Replacement Obligations
Portland’s Tree Code prioritizes "no net loss" of canopy. If a permit is granted for removal, you will likely have a **mitigation** requirement.

* **Tree-for-Tree:** Most standard removals (trees 12–20" DBH, or trees that are dead/dying/dangerous) require you to plant **one** replacement tree on the property.
* **Inch-for-Inch:** If you are removing a large, healthy tree ($\ge 20$ inches DBH), the city may require "inch-for-inch" replacement, meaning the total diameter of new trees planted must match the diameter of the tree removed.
* **Fee-in-Lieu:** If there is no physical space to replant, you can pay a fee into the **City’s Tree Planting and Preservation Fund**. 

### **Next Steps for Your Property**
1.  **Measure the Tree:** Use a tape measure to find the circumference at $4.5$ feet up the trunk and divide by $\pi$ ($3.14$) to get the DBH.
2.  **Check PortlandMaps:** Go to [PortlandMaps.com](https://www.portlandmaps.com), enter your address, and look at the **"Trees"** or **"Zoning"** layers to confirm if the tree is officially cataloged as a street tree.
3.  **Apply Online:** You can submit your application through the [Portland Permitting & Development (DevHub) portal](https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-permits).

> [!TIP]
> Since Glenfair has been part of recent city-funded planting initiatives, you may be eligible for a **free replacement tree** through the "Trees for Streams" or "Urban Forestry" planting programs if you are required to replant.

**Size and height**
- Max ADU size: 800 sq ft *or* 75% of the main house's living area, whichever is smaller (the basement-conversion exception waives the 75% cap if the primary structure is 5+ years old).
- Height: 20 ft outside required setbacks; 15 ft if any portion is inside the setback (with a 10 ft exterior wall cap there).
- Detached ADU building coverage can't exceed the primary structure's coverage; all detached accessory structures combined can't exceed 20% of the site.

**Setbacks**
- Side and rear: 5 ft minimum (can be larger depending on base zone; verify per address).
- Front: detached ADU must be at least 40 ft from the front lot line, *or* located behind the rear wall of the main house.
- Corner lots: at least 20 ft from the side street lot line.

**The 2025 design rule change worth knowing**
Portland now splits ADUs into two tiers, which is a meaningful planning lever:
- **Under 500 sq ft:** no design-matching to the main house required; can be built within setbacks; flat or non-conforming roofs allowed; siding/window proportions are free. Big win if you want a modern detached studio.
- **500-800 sq ft:** still subject to design standards (siding pattern, roof pitch matching, etc.) once over 15 ft tall.

**Up to two ADUs per lot** are now allowed in most residential zones (with house, attached house, or manufactured home); only one can be attached. Duplexes can have one detached ADU.

**Permits and process**
- Apply through Portland Permitting & Development; ADU permit process is online.
- Contractor must have an active Oregon CCB license; you cannot DIY the electrical work (a licensed electrical contractor is required by state law), though an owner can sign as their own contractor for the rest with the Owner Doing Work form.
- Pre-approved detached ADU plans are available from the city and can speed review meaningfully.
- Typical construction timeline: 4-6 months after permit issuance, on top of design and permitting time.

**Money: two SDC programs, easy to confuse**
- **ADU SDC Waiver** (the long-running one): waives system development charges (typically $12,000-$19,000 per ADU). Requires a 10-year covenant that neither the ADU nor the main house will be used as a short-term rental. Violating costs 150% of current SDC rates.
- **Temporary SDC Exemption** (passed July 2025, ordinance 192082): exempts most new housing units from SDCs for permits issued **Aug 15, 2025 through Sept 30, 2028**. Important catch: per the city's own FAQ, this temporary exemption *does not apply to ADUs* (ADUs continue under their separate waiver program). So the new program doesn't stack; ADU builders should still go through the ADU-specific waiver.

---

**Sellwood-Moreland-specific things worth checking before you commit to any property in this neighborhood** (these often get missed):

1. **Flood plain / Willamette Greenway.** Lots west of about SE 13th, and especially anything near Oaks Bottom or the Sellwood Riverfront, can fall within FEMA Special Flood Hazard Areas or the Willamette Greenway overlay (the "g" overlay), both of which add review and elevation requirements. The address you gave is east of the river by several blocks so probably clear, but worth verifying on PortlandMaps' Hazards layer.
2. **Tree code.** Title 11 protects trees by size; a mature tree in the proposed ADU footprint can derail siting or trigger mitigation fees that materially change the budget.
3. **Constrained Sites overlay ("z").** If a property carries the "z" overlay, second ADUs are not allowed; only one. Worth checking before assuming the two-ADU rule applies.
4. **Parking.** Portland eliminated minimum off-street parking requirements citywide, so an ADU does not trigger a new parking requirement; this is one of the few things that's actually simpler than people expect.
5. **Sewer line condition.** Sellwood has a lot of pre-1950 housing stock with original clay laterals. An ADU requires sufficient sewer capacity, and a failing main lateral can mean a $10-20k surprise. Worth a sewer scope before design, not after.

If you can tell me what's behind the question (different property, considering buying, just exploring), I can dig deeper into the specific scenario; the answer changes a lot depending on what's actually on the lot.

This changes the feasibility significantly. ADU rules in Portland primarily apply to properties with a primary single-family house, attached house, manufactured home, or (in some cases) duplex. They are not typically intended for adding a separate ADU to an existing multi-family apartment complex. Confirm the exact current zoning and ownership details for the parcel(s) you have in mind using the official Portland Zoning Map tool or PortlandMaps.com by searching the address.

### Determining Your Property's Zoning
- Use the interactive **Portland Zoning map** at portlandmaps.com/bps/zoning/: Search for "5955 SE Milwaukie Ave, Portland, OR 97202," zoom to the parcel, and click for base zone (e.g., R5, RM#, CM#, etc.), overlay zones, plan districts, lot size, and other details.
- Sellwood-Moreland (especially residential portions) commonly features single-dwelling zones like **R5** (Residential 5,000), with some multi-dwelling (RM) or commercial/mixed-use zones (CM) along corridors like Milwaukie Ave. Nearby or related properties have been rezoned or developed under multi-dwelling standards in the past.
- Check for overlays (e.g., historic, environmental, constrained sites "z"), flood/slope hazards, or neighborhood plan influences via PortlandMaps.com. These can add restrictions or review requirements.

If the property is in a single-dwelling zone (RF–R2.5) with a qualifying primary structure and meets minimum lot size, an ADU is likely feasible (subject to all standards). In multi-dwelling or commercial zones, allowances differ and may be more restrictive for adding a separate unit to an existing apartment building. Contact the Bureau of Development Services (BDS) Zoning Information Line (503-823-7526) or schedule a free 15-minute consultation for site-specific confirmation.

### General ADU Standards in Portland (from Zoning Code Chapter 33.205)
These apply across most residential zones unless noted; exact rules depend on your base zone (single-dwelling vs. multi-dwelling). Always verify against the current code and your property.

**Size Limits**:
- Maximum ADU size is generally **75% of the primary structure's living area or 800 sq ft, whichever is smaller**. (Example: 1,000 sq ft primary house → max ~750 sq ft ADU; larger houses cap at 800 sq ft.)
- Some exceptions or adjustments: Basement conversions (in older homes) may allow up to 1,000 sq ft; limited adjustments possible via review for slightly larger sizes in some cases. On larger lots, caps can reach 1,000 sq ft in certain contexts.
- All detached accessory structures (including the ADU) generally limited to ≤20% site coverage. The ADU's building coverage cannot exceed that of the primary structure.

**Setbacks and Placement**:
- **Front**: Detached ADUs must be set back at least **40 feet** from the front lot line **or** placed behind the rear wall of the primary structure.
- **Side and rear**: Typically start at **5 feet** minimum, but can be larger depending on the specific base zone and development standards (e.g., per Chapters 33.110 or 33.120). Use the Detached Covered Accessory Structures Worksheet for details.
- Height within required setbacks is limited to 15 feet; outside setbacks, up to 20 feet max for detached ADUs.

**Height and Design**:
- Max 20 ft (outside setbacks) or 15 ft (inside).
- For detached ADUs >15 ft tall: Must match the primary structure in materials, roof pitch, trim, eaves, and window orientation (specific standards in code).
- No additional off-street parking required for the ADU.

**Number of ADUs**:
- Generally **one ADU** on a lot with a qualifying primary house (more flexible in some zones).
- Up to **two ADUs** allowed in many R20–R2.5 zones (with conditions like city street frontage, minimum lot size, no constrained sites overlay, and visitability requirements for at least one unit).
- Fewer or different rules apply to duplexes or multi-dwelling properties.

**Minimum Lot Sizes** (for one ADU with primary structure in single-dwelling zones; examples):
- R5: Often around 2,500 sq ft (varies; check Table 205-1/205-2).
- Exact thresholds differ by zone and whether you have a house vs. attached house/multiple units. Larger minimums may apply for two ADUs or attached houses.

Other rules cover visitability (wheelchair access features in some cases), short-term rental restrictions (with SDC implications), and historic/overlay compliance.

### Permit Process
1. **Research**: Check zoning, lot size, permit history (unpermitted prior work must be addressed), hazards (flood, slope via PortlandMaps), utilities, and trees. Use the Detached Accessory Structures Worksheet and review Chapter 33.205.
2. **Pre-Application**: Schedule free 15-minute BDS appointments (planner, building reviewer, etc.) for guidance.
3. **Apply**: Submit building permit (and trade permits for electrical/plumbing/mechanical) online via **Development Hub PDX**. Include site plans, architectural/structural drawings. New detached ADUs follow single-family residence guidelines; conversions have specific code guides. Pre-approved ADU plans are available for some designs to speed review.
4. **Review & Corrections**: Plans are reviewed; respond to any checksheets.
5. **Permit Issuance & Fees**: Pay building permit fees + any System Development Charges (SDCs). **SDC waiver** possible for 10-year covenant against short-term rentals.
6. **Construction & Inspections**: Install erosion control/tree protection as needed. Schedule inspections; obtain final occupancy approval.

Contractors must be licensed (CCB for most work). Homeowners can pull some permits but not electrical. The process emphasizes compliance with Oregon Residential Specialty Code for new builds or specific ADU conversion guides.

**Key Resources**:
- Official ADU zoning page: portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-code-overview/adu-zoning (Chapter 33.205).
- Permitting page: portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/accessory-dwelling-units.
- Zoning map: portlandmaps.com/bps/zoning/.
- BDS contact for early assistance.
