Let me verify the current Portland regulations on this — given the building's age and historic status, I want to make sure I'm pointing you at the right rules.# Short answer
**Yes, you need a permit.** This is a commercial building (former church, currently the SE VFW hall, owned by Southeast VFW Building LLC), so it doesn't qualify for any of the residential reroof exemptions. A building permit is required to repair, alter or change occupancy or use of an existing commercial structure.

The most common path for a like-for-like commercial reroof is the City's **Commercial Reroof Label Program**: a streamlined, lower-cost option where a registered roofing contractor pre-purchases up to five reroof labels, then assigns one to your address. A commercial building permit may be required instead of being able to use a reroof label when the project is more involved (significant sheathing replacement, structural changes, material change, etc.).

If you're hiring it out, the contractor will know which path applies; tell them the address and they'll handle it. If they're not already registered for the label program, the alternative is a regular commercial building permit through PP&D (formerly BDS).

# What makes this property special

I pulled the PortlandMaps record so you'd have the full picture, because this address has more going on than a typical SE Portland building.

## Property profile (PortlandMaps R275995)

- **Historic identity**: This is the **First Free Methodist Episcopal Church**, built circa 1893 (the assessor's "1919" likely reflects a major remodel). Currently classified as a **"Significant Resource"** on Portland's Historic Resources Inventory ([Oregon SHPO record #51232](https://heritagedata.prd.state.or.us/historic/index.cfm?do=v.dsp_siteSummary&resultDisplay=51232)).
- **Base zoning**: IG1 (General Industrial 1)
- **Plan district**: Central City > Central Eastside subdistrict
- **Comprehensive plan**: Industrial Sanctuary
- **Urban renewal area**: Central Eastside Corridor (eligible)
- **Overlays**: None (importantly, no "d" design overlay)
- **Lot**: 4,209 sqft; recent occupancy A-3 Assembly, construction type V-B
- **Hazards**: Moderate liquefaction; Portland Hills Fault nearby (violent shaking potential)
- **Council district**: 3

## Zoning, age, and overlay implications for a reroof

**Historic status (the big one):** This is where most people get the answer wrong. "Significant Resource" sounds like it would trigger Historic Resource Review, but it doesn't, at least not for alterations like reroofing. Demolition of Significant Resources requires 120-Day Demolition Delay. The specific historic preservation land use regulations that apply to Significant Resources can be found in Portland Zoning Code 33.445 Sections 320 through 340. Alteration review (HRR) only kicks in for Historic Landmarks, Conservation Landmarks, National Register Landmarks, and contributing structures inside designated districts; this property is none of those. So no HRR for a reroof.

(Even if it were in a Historic District: No for typical asphalt composition shingle or other original roofing replacement, which is considered repair under City Code. This includes replacement of the plywood sheathing under the roofing materials. A material change (e.g., metal to composition, or composition to membrane) would be the trigger.)

**IG1 / Central Eastside / no d-overlay:** No Design Review either. The plan district sets character expectations for new construction and major alterations, not roof replacement.

**Age (1893/1919):** The thing that actually matters here is **asbestos**. Oregon DEQ requires an accredited-inspector asbestos survey before renovation or demolition of any building constructed before 2004, and built-up roofing, mastics, flashing cement, and roofing felts in buildings of this era frequently contain asbestos. The City flags this on its commercial permit page: For asbestos abatement, contact the Oregon Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ) or call them (800) 452-4011. Don't skip this; DEQ fines for unsurveyed work are real, and a reputable commercial roofer will usually require the survey before they'll bid.

**Energy code:** Commercial reroofs that exceed certain thresholds can trigger roof insulation upgrades under the Oregon Energy Efficiency Specialty Code. Worth asking the contractor about explicitly, especially on a flat or low-slope roof.

**Seismic / Title 24.85:** A pure roof covering replacement typically doesn't trigger seismic upgrade requirements, but if any structural roof framing work is involved on a building this old, it can. Since the building is unreinforced or partially-reinforced masonry-era construction in a moderate liquefaction zone with the Portland Hills Fault next door, this is worth a deliberate scope conversation rather than a discovery after demo.

# Things you might not have asked but probably want to know

Two angles that go beyond the permit question, since you asked about this specific building:

**1. Significant Resource status is also a quiet opportunity.** A 1893 former Free Methodist church is exactly the kind of building eligible for upgrade to **Historic Landmark** or **National Register** designation (owner-initiated; owner consent required in Oregon). That unlocks the **Oregon Special Assessment of Historic Property Program**, which freezes assessed value for 10 years in exchange for a substantial rehab. Established in 1975, the Special Assessment of Historic Property Program provides a 10-year "freeze" on the assessed value of National Register-listed properties when an owner commits to a substantial rehabilitation project. If the VFW post is contemplating a bigger restoration arc and not just a reroof, this is worth a 15-minute conversation with the City's Historic Resources program before the work starts (designation has to be in place before the rehab to qualify).

**2. The "matching material" point matters even though HRR doesn't apply.** If this building ever does get upgraded to Landmark / National Register, the prior reroof's material choice becomes part of the conversation. Replacing whatever's there now with something visually similar (and ideally documenting what was removed) preserves future options at no extra cost today.

# Practical next steps

1. Confirm the existing roof material and look up prior roof permits via PortlandMaps' permit search (I checked: no reroof permits on record for this property, only kitchen/electrical/sign work from 2016-2019; the last roof work predates the digital records or was done without a permit).
2. Get the asbestos survey done by a DEQ-accredited inspector before bid finalization.
3. Have your roofer either use a Commercial Reroof Label or apply for a commercial building permit through PP&D's [Development Hub PDX](https://www.portland.gov/ppd).
4. Optional: book a free 15-minute pre-application appointment with PP&D if scope is unclear; they'll tell you definitively which permit path applies.
5. Optional but worth a thought: contact Portland's Historic Resources program ([portland.gov/bps/historic-resources](https://www.portland.gov/bps/historic-resources)) before any work if landmark designation might be on the table.

One caveat: I'm pulling from PortlandMaps and current City code summaries, but the definitive call on permit path always comes from PP&D for the specific scope of work. The phone number for general permit questions is 503-823-7300.
